1.synthesis:
Samuel comes to Saul with a direct divine command: destroy the Amalekites utterly — men, women, children, infant, ox, sheep, camel, donkey. The command is grounded in historical memory: the Amalekites opposed Israel when they came up from Egypt (Exod 17:8–16; Deut 25:17–19). This is a herem command — total consecration to destruction, the most complete form of holy war. Saul musters two hundred thousand infantry from Israel and ten thousand from Judah. He gives warning to the Kenites, who had shown kindness to Israel at the Exodus, to move away before the attack. Then he attacks the Amalekites from Havilah to Shur, east of Egypt. Saul and the army spare Agag the Amalekite king and the best of the sheep, cattle, fat calves, and lambs — everything that is good. Everything despised and weak they totally destroy.

The disobedience is precisely calibrated: Saul does what the command says (destroy the Amalekites) but keeps what the command prohibits (Agag and the best livestock). The distinctions he draws — "everything good" spared, "everything despised" destroyed — reveal the heart of the problem. The herem command was designed to remove Israel from the temptation of economic benefit from holy war; Saul applies it to what has no economic value and ignores it for what does. John Gill observes that the pattern is familiar from Genesis: the first sin involved taking what was forbidden while leaving what was permitted; Saul's sin here reverses the command in exactly the same fashion — taking the pleasant, leaving the unprofitable.

Keil and Delitzsch note the kindness shown to the Kenites as a sign that the command is not arbitrary genocide but a specific judgment against a specific historical enemy who attacked God's people at their most vulnerable moment. The Kenites, who allied with Israel, are exempted. The command has moral logic within the covenant framework, even if its application is difficult for contemporary readers.
1.historical_context: The Amalekites' attack on Israel in Exod 17:8–16 targeted the stragglers at the rear of the Exodus procession — the most vulnerable — which is why Deut 25:17–18 specifies that they "attacked you on the march when you were faint and weary." The command for total destruction in Deut 25:19 was explicitly connected to when Israel "rest from all your enemies around you in the land." Saul's campaign fulfills the long-delayed command. The Kenites (15:6) were associated with Moses's father-in-law Jethro (Judg 1:16); their exemption demonstrates that the command has a specific historical target, not a general ethnic logic. Agag, the Amalekite king, being spared suggests Saul followed the ancient Near Eastern custom of displaying captive kings as trophies of victory — a prestige move explicitly prohibited by the herem command.
1.literary_structure: The section moves from command (15:1–3) through preparation and partial kindness (15:4–6) to the act of disobedience (15:7–9). The disobedience is narrated without editorializing — the reader sees what Saul did and draws the conclusion. The phrase "everything that was good" (15:9) is the key: Saul's criterion was economic, not covenantal.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry reads the Amalek command as a test of whether Saul will obey a difficult, costly divine command rather than a convenient one. The livestock spared was real economic value — what the army had "gone to war" to acquire in the ancient economy of plunder. Full obedience of the herem command would have cost Saul and his army significant resources. The test is precisely whether obedience will be maintained when it costs something tangible. Calvin observes that partial obedience is not obedience at all when the specific prohibited thing is retained: a command to destroy something is not fulfilled by destroying the unprofitable parts. The church faces the same test whenever obedience to God's word requires surrendering something of genuine value.
1.christology: The herem command, with its sweeping totality, raises deep questions about divine violence that the New Testament addresses christologically. Christ bears the herem — the totality of divine judgment — on himself. "He himself bore our sins in his body on the cross" (1 Pet 2:24); "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us" (Gal 3:13). The total destruction that the herem demands falls on Christ rather than on Israel's enemies in the New Covenant — the principle of substitutionary atonement transforms the Old Testament herem from an outward act of military judgment to an inward act of redemptive grace. What God's holiness required in type — the total destruction of the enemy — is accomplished in antitype through the destruction of sin's power in the one who became sin for us.

10.synthesis:
The Lord speaks to Samuel in the night: "I regret that I have made Saul king, because he has turned away from me and has not carried out my instructions." Samuel is troubled and cries out to the Lord all night. In the morning he goes to meet Saul, who has gone to Carmel to set up a monument for himself and then turned to Gilgal. When Samuel arrives, Saul greets him: "The Lord bless you! I have carried out the Lord's instructions." The contrast between the self-congratulation of the greeting and the reality of the situation is the narrative's sharpest irony. Samuel replies: "What then is this bleating of sheep in my ears? What is this lowing of cattle that I hear?" Saul's defense has three stages: the people spared the best livestock to sacrifice to the Lord; I did obey the Lord and went on the mission; I destroyed all the Amalekites; we brought back Agag and the livestock for sacrifices. Each response diminishes Saul's responsibility — the people are blamed, the positive obedience is emphasized, the sacrifice is offered as justification for the disobedience.

Samuel's response is one of the most famous prophetic declarations in Scripture: "Does the Lord delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as much as in obeying the Lord? To obey is better than sacrifice, and to heed is better than the fat of rams. For rebellion is like the sin of divination, and arrogance like the evil of idolatry. Because you have rejected the word of the Lord, he has rejected you as king." Keil and Delitzsch observe that this passage establishes the prophetic principle that will govern the entire prophetic movement from Samuel through Malachi: the ritual system exists to serve covenant faithfulness, not substitute for it. Sacrifices offered in the context of disobedience are not more but less than nothing — they are a form of manipulation of the divine presence.

John Gill notes the escalating gravity of Samuel's indictment: rebellion is not merely disobedience but is categorized with divination (an attempt to manipulate divine power); arrogance is categorized with idolatry (a substitution of self-will for God-worship). Saul's sin is not merely practical disobedience but a theological orientation — treating God's commands as negotiable raw material to be worked with rather than as sovereign instructions to be obeyed. Matthew Henry observes that Samuel's grief through the night before this confrontation is the grief of a faithful pastor who loves the person he must condemn.
10.historical_context: The monument Saul erects for himself at Carmel (15:12) is the first indication in the text that Saul's primary orientation has shifted from service to self-glorification. Setting up a stele or monument was the ancient Near Eastern equivalent of writing one's name in lights; doing so at Carmel, before returning to Gilgal (the site of Samuel's authority), suggests Saul is rehearsing his own triumph independently of the covenant framework Samuel represents. The prophetic principle "to obey is better than sacrifice" (15:22) will be echoed in Hos 6:6 ("I desire mercy, not sacrifice"), Isa 1:11–17, Amos 5:21–24, and cited by Jesus himself in Matt 9:13.
10.literary_structure: The confrontation follows the prophetic judgment-oracle pattern: indictment (the bleating of sheep, 15:14), defense and deflection (Saul's three-stage excuse, 15:15), counter-claim (Samuel's interruption of the deflection, 15:16–19), further defense (15:20–21), and verdict (15:22–23). The sheep bleating in the background is the physical evidence that makes Saul's self-defense impossible from the moment Samuel arrives.
10.theological_application: Calvin, commenting on "to obey is better than sacrifice," establishes that this principle governs the entire Old Testament sacrificial system: the sacrifices were always intended to express and reinforce covenant faithfulness, not to provide a mechanism for managing covenant violations through ritual payment. When sacrifice is offered in the context of deliberate, unrepented disobedience, it is not elevated worship but an attempt to purchase God's approval without the cost of obedience. This is the root of all false religion: finding a way to have God's benefits without surrendering to God's authority. The contemporary forms are infinite — church attendance, charitable giving, public religious performance — offered in the hope that they will balance the ledger of genuine disobedience. Samuel's verdict is that they do not.
10.christology: Samuel's teaching that God desires obedience rather than sacrifice is fulfilled in Christ from two directions. Christ fulfills the direction of perfect obedience: "He humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross!" (Phil 2:8). And Christ fulfills the direction of perfect sacrifice: his obedience is itself the sacrifice, the life given in perfect submission to the Father's will. The cross is not obedience-replacing-sacrifice or sacrifice-replacing-obedience but obedience as sacrifice — the fulfillment of Samuel's principle in its deepest sense. The writer to the Hebrews applies this directly: "Then I said, 'Here I am — it is written about me in the scroll — I have come to do your will, my God'" (Heb 10:7; citing Ps 40:6–8), in the context of Christ's single sacrifice superseding the entire sacrificial system.

24.synthesis:
After Samuel's verdict, Saul confesses: "I have sinned. I violated the Lord's command and your instructions. I was afraid of the men and so I gave in to them." The confession names the sin and identifies the motive — fear of human opinion. But what follows reveals the confession's limitation: Saul asks Samuel to pardon his sin and come back with him, explicitly so that he may worship the Lord. The request is for Samuel's presence, for the public appearance of the prophet's continuing support, rather than for genuine reconciliation with God. Samuel refuses: "I will not go back with you. You have rejected the word of the Lord, and the Lord has rejected you as king over Israel." As Samuel turns to leave, Saul grabs the edge of his robe and tears it. Samuel uses the torn garment as an object lesson: the Lord has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and given it to one of your neighbors — to one better than you.

Saul confesses a second time — "I have sinned" — but adds: "Please honor me before the elders of my people and before Israel; come back with me, so that I may worship the Lord your God." The honor request is nakedly self-interested: Saul wants Samuel's presence not for spiritual restoration but for political face-saving. Samuel makes a partial concession — he does go back with Saul, and he does honor Saul before the people by executing Agag himself ("As your sword has made women childless, so will your mother be childless among women"). But the breach between Saul and Samuel is final: Samuel does not go to see Saul again to the day of his death, though he mourns for him. And the Lord regrets that he had made Saul king over Israel.

Keil and Delitzsch distinguish between Saul's two confessions: the first ("I have sinned") has the form of genuine confession; the second — immediately adding "honor me before my people" — reveals the heart's actual orientation. Matthew Henry identifies the torn robe as a prophetic sign that the torn kingdom will not be rejoined: the Lord has made this permanent.
24.historical_context: The torn garment as prophetic sign appears elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible: Ahijah's torn garment signifies the division of Solomon's kingdom (1 Kgs 11:29–31). Saul's accidental tearing of Samuel's robe becomes the occasion for an unplanned prophetic act — the Lord uses Saul's desperate physical gesture to deliver the confirming sign of the transfer of the kingdom. The execution of Agag by Samuel — hewing him in pieces before the Lord (15:33) — fulfills the herem command that Saul failed to execute; Samuel acts as both prophet and judge, doing what the king refused to do. The statement that "Samuel grieved for Saul" even after rejecting him captures the pastoral complexity of prophetic ministry: the prophet can simultaneously pronounce judgment and mourn the person who receives it.
24.literary_structure: The section moves through three exchanges: the first confession and refusal (15:24–26), the robe tearing and its interpretation (15:27–29), and the second confession and partial concession (15:30–31). The Agag execution (15:32–33) and the final separation (15:34–35) close the sequence. The structure reveals how Saul's apparent penitence is actually a series of negotiations for face-saving.
24.theological_application: Saul's confession that he sinned because he "feared the people" is among the most honest self-diagnoses in the Old Testament — and one of the most recognizable. The fear of human opinion is the consistent root of Saul's failures: it drove the unlawful sacrifice (the people are scattering), it drove the rash oath (battle momentum), and it drives the Amalek disobedience. Calvin observes that the sin of people-pleasing corrupts every other virtue because it substitutes the crowd's evaluation for God's authority. The person who leads or serves from fear of people's opinion cannot sustain obedience when the people's opinion diverges from God's command — as it regularly does. The post-confession request for honor reveals that even in the moment of confession, Saul's concern is for what others think of him rather than for what God thinks of his heart.
24.christology: Samuel's mourning for Saul — the prophet who must reject, yet grieves — anticipates Christ weeping over Jerusalem: "Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you, how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing" (Matt 23:37). Divine judgment and divine grief coexist in the heart of the one who must execute it. The Lord's regret over making Saul king is not a revision of divine sovereignty but the expression of God's genuine emotional engagement with human choices and their consequences. The God who regrets does not become a different God; he is the God who, in regretting, demonstrates that he is not indifferent to human failure but takes it with full seriousness — a seriousness that will ultimately lead him to bear that failure himself in the cross.
